text.title: Architectural Patterns: Layered, Clean, Hexagonal
text.summary: Three domain-centric architectural patterns for organizing application code: Layered (traditional, horizontal), Vertical Slice (feature-first), Clean/Hexagonal (dependency-inversion, ports and adapters). Each enforces a dependency rule between layers.

rule.statement: Use Layered Architecture for traditional CRUD web applications where the domain logic is thin and team familiarity with the pattern is high. Enforce a strict dependency rule: each layer may only depend on the layer directly below it.
rule.rationale: Layered architecture is the most widely understood pattern, minimizing onboarding friction. However, it encourages "anemic domain models" when business logic leaks into the service layer and then the repository layer.

list.item: Presentation: controllers, views, DTOs, API endpoints
Application: use cases, services, orchestration, transaction coordination
Domain: entities, value objects, business rules, domain services
Infrastructure: repositories, ORM adapters, external API clients, messaging

antipattern.description: Business logic in the database (stored procedures) or in controllers
antipattern.reason: Makes the domain untestable without a running database and couples business rules to infrastructure details

rule.statement: Use Clean or Hexagonal Architecture when the domain is complex and must be testable independently of the database, framework, and external APIs. The dependency rule is absolute: inner layers never import outer layers; outer layers depend on inner via abstractions (ports).
rule.rationale: Dependency inversion at the architectural level means the domain can be tested with in-memory repositories and mock adapters, reducing integration test surface. Framework and database upgrades do not touch domain logic.

list.item: Entities: enterprise business rules, stable core, no external dependencies
Use Cases: application-specific business rules, orchestrate entities
Interface Adapters: controllers, gateways, presenters — convert from use-case format to external format
Frameworks and Drivers: web frameworks, databases, UI — the outermost ring, most volatile
Ports: abstract interfaces defined by the domain, implemented by adapters (Hexagonal variant)

example.note: Dependency direction: all arrows point inward
example.code: PostgresUserRepository → implements → UserRepository (port in domain) DjangoController → calls → CreateUserUseCase (application) CreateUserUseCase → uses → User (entity) + UserRepository (port) User (entity) → depends on → nothing external

rule.statement: Use Vertical Slice Architecture when features are developed by small cross-functional teams and the priority is minimizing merge conflicts and maximizing feature cohesion. Each slice owns its full stack from HTTP handler to database query.
rule.rationale: Horizontal layers cause high coupling across features (changes to the user service cascade across all layers). Vertical slices contain change: adding a feature means adding a new slice, not modifying existing shared layers.

list.item: Each feature folder contains its own handler, service, repository, and DTOs
Cross-cutting concerns (auth, logging, tracing) are handled by middleware, not by shared service layers
Slices share the database schema but not application code
Trade-off: some code duplication across slices in exchange for isolated changeability
